topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed on my monitor, a stark testament to hours spent tracking departmental expenditures. I clicked on the payroll summary, and a sudden, cold knot tightened in my stomach. It was the quarterly review, and the numbers… they didn't align with my expectations. I scrolled down, my breath catching in my throat as I saw the figures for the junior analyst, Mark. His salary, when cross-referenced with his project load, was significantly higher than mine. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in my chest. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared blankly at the wall, meticulously cataloging the dust motes dancing in the afternoon light. When Mark walked by, offering a bright, unwarranted smile, I managed a clipped, overly cheerful, "Good afternoon, Mark." The forced lightness felt brittle on my tongue.

[story 2]

I reread the email three times, the fluorescent lights above my cubicle buzzing with an aggressive monotony. The data was undeniable: the compensation structure for the junior team was skewed in a way that felt fundamentally unfair. A tightness clamped around my jaw, making my molars ache. I picked up my pen, intending to make a note, but my hand trembled slightly, the ink threatening to bleed across the page. I glanced over at Sarah, who was laughing loudly with a colleague across the aisle. Her easy confidence seemed to amplify my own internal tension. I cleared my throat, the sound grating in the quiet office, and forced a neutral tone, "Just confirming the Q3 projections are accurate." Every syllable was carefully measured, a deliberate effort to keep the rising pressure contained beneath a veneer of professional calm.

[story 3]

My gaze locked onto the screen, the numbers flashing in a sequence that made my vision swim momentarily. It was a stark reality, laid bare by the HR reports. A distinct feeling of vexation washed over me, heavy and unwelcome. I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles turned white. I started tapping my fingers rapidly against the laminate surface, a frantic, uneven rhythm that no one else seemed to notice. I stood up abruptly, stretching my arms above my head, the movement sharp and jerky. As I turned back to my desk, I noticed David approaching, his expression open and friendly. I met his eyes, offering a tight, brief nod. "Everything alright?" I asked, the flatness in my voice betraying the internal upheaval churning beneath the surface.
